metadata.title: Why 48 Iron Atoms Make Quantum Ripples Nobody Placed There
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one accent. TEAL = standing-wave modes / quantum / fitting; CRIMSON = classical smooth distribution / non-fitting waves
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: Amharic. Medhavy 1-word budget. Wagwan is Bear-only.
metadata.audio_note: All existing inner-beat mp3s were generated with ElevenLabs NBB voice. Must regenerate ALL with kokoro af_kore for Medhavy: python3 runtime/scripts/generate_audio.py quantum-mechanics-vol1/youtube/vox-quantum-corral

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. IBM arranged 48 iron atoms into a 7-nanometer ring that acts as a wall, not a source. Surface electrons bounce off the wall, and their wave functions must vanish at the boundary. Only standing waves that fit inside the ring survive; the rest cancel and are forbidden. The surviving discrete modes have fixed radial nodes that appear as the STM's concentric rings.

artifactLines: IBM arranged 48 iron atoms into a 7-nanometer ring that acts as a wall, not a source.
Surface electrons bounce off the wall, and their wave functions must vanish at the boundary.
Only standing waves that fit inside the ring survive; the rest cancel and are forbidden.
The surviving discrete modes have fixed radial nodes that appear as the STM's concentric rings.

beats.narration_text: What stunned me is that IBM placed 48 iron atoms as a wall and walked away, and the boundary condition alone forced discrete standing waves with fixed radial nodes into the STM image. I want to reproduce the geometry. Please: (1) show how requiring the wave function to vanish on the 7-nanometer ring selects the allowed modes, (2) confirm the roughly 14-nanometer ground-state wavelength as one half-wave across the diameter, and (3) predict where the next mode's extra bright ring should appear.

props.segment: Why 48 Iron Atoms Make Quantum Ripples Nobody Placed There
props.command: What stunned me is that IBM placed 48 iron atoms as a wall and walked away, and the boundary condition alone forced discrete standing waves with fixed radial nodes into the STM image. I want to reproduce the geometry. Please: (1) show how requiring the wave function to vanish on the 7-nanometer ring selects the allowed modes, (2) confirm the roughly 14-nanometer ground-state wavelength as one half-wave across the diameter, and (3) predict where the next mode's extra bright ring should appear.

beats.narration_text: In 1993, IBM physicists arranged 48 iron atoms in a ring on a copper surface. Inside the ring, the STM image showed concentric ripples — rings of electron density nobody put there. Quantum mechanics put them there.

card.copy: Why 48 Iron Atoms Make Quantum Ripples Nobody Placed There

props.segment: Why 48 Iron Atoms Make Quantum Ripples Nobody Placed There
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: 48 iron atoms arranged in a circle on a 2D surface, labeled as a 7 nm ring — show electrons inside the ring as freely moving dots that bounce off the atom wall"

beats.narration_text: The 48 iron atoms form a ring about 7 nanometers across. They are not a source of electrons. They are a wall. The electrons on the copper surface move freely — until they hit this wall. And then the wave bounces back.

Classical electrons in a circular pen would distribute smoothly. The STM image shows sharp rings at fixed radii. These rings were not placed. They appeared from confinement alone. Where do they come from?

card.copy:
Classical: smooth distribution inside.
Quantum corral: sharp rings appear.
Where do the rings come from?

props.segment: Why 48 Iron Atoms Make Quantum Ripples Nobody Placed There
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: two circular standing wave patterns inside a ring — one that fits perfectly (wave function zero at boundary) and one that doesn't fit (nonzero at boundary) — label 'allowed' and 'forbidden'"

beats.narration_text: The electron's wave function must vanish at the ring boundary — it can't pass through the iron atoms. Of all the possible standing waves, only those whose pattern fits inside the ring survive. Any wave that doesn't fit cancels itself at the boundary and is forbidden.

props.segment: Why 48 Iron Atoms Make Quantum Ripples Nobody Placed There
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: discrete radial modes inside a circular corral — first mode (ground state): single central peak; second mode: central peak plus one ring; third mode: central peak plus two rings — show them in sequence"

beats.narration_text: The surviving modes are discrete. The first mode has one central peak — the ground state. The second mode has a central peak and one ring. Each mode has a fixed number of radial nodes, and those nodes show up as dark rings in the STM image.

props.segment: Why 48 Iron Atoms Make Quantum Ripples Nobody Placed There
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: a 7 nm diameter circle with wavelength fitting shown — ground state: one half-wavelength across the diameter, ~14 nm wavelength labeled; show the bright central peak position"

beats.narration_text: The radii of the bright rings are set by the circumference of the corral and the electron's wavelength. For the 7-nanometer corral, the ground-state wavelength that fits is about 14 nanometers — one half-wavelength across the diameter. That fixes where the bright peak appears.

props.segment: Why 48 Iron Atoms Make Quantum Ripples Nobody Placed There
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: guitar string with fixed ends on the left showing only integer harmonics surviving — corral with fixed boundary on the right showing only fitting radial modes surviving — label the analogy"

beats.narration_text: The key point: the experimenters did not put these rings there. They placed 48 iron atoms in a circle and walked away. The quantum boundary condition produced the rings automatically — the same way a guitar string produces specific harmonics because its ends are fixed.

props.segment: Why 48 Iron Atoms Make Quantum Ripples Nobody Placed There
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: quantum dot, semiconductor well, and atom side by side — each showing discrete energy levels from boundary conditions, with the corral as the visible prototype"

beats.narration_text: The quantum corral is not a special phenomenon. It is just quantization made visible. Every quantum dot, every semiconductor well, every atom — they all have discrete states because their boundary conditions forbid everything else. The corral made this literal: you can see it.

props.segment: Why 48 Iron Atoms Make Quantum Ripples Nobody Placed There
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: the Crommie-Lutz-Eigler 1993 corral image as a simulated STM topograph — concentric rings of electron density inside the iron atom ring, labeled Science 1993"

beats.narration_text: Crommie, Lutz, and Eigler published this image in Science in 1993. It became one of the most reproduced images in quantum physics. Not because it is beautiful — though it is — but because it shows the wave function of electrons, not as math, but as a photograph.
48 iron atoms. A wall. Only modes that fit survive. Rings appear from boundary conditions alone. The quantum corral is quantization made visible.

card.copy:
Ring wall: only fitting modes survive.
Discrete nodes = rings in the image.
Quantization made visible (1993).

beats.narration_text: Those ripples are standing waves, and they appear automatically. Paste this into Claude and build the corral.

props.segment: Why 48 Iron Atoms Make Quantum Ripples Nobody Placed There
props.command: claude "simulate a quantum corral: 48 iron atoms in a ring on a 2D electron gas surface, show the standing wave pattern that emerges inside using the electron density"
